claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules so I can guide you properly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a child, Alex, who's 6 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few more questions.

Do you live with Alex in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me. Actually, I don't live in the UK myself at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. This is important information for determining eligibility.

Can you tell me which country you currently live in?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm living abroad at the moment, outside the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand you're living abroad. To properly assess your eligibility, I need to know the specific country you're living in, as some countries may allow you to still claim Child Benefit (for example, if you're a Crown servant or living in certain countries with agreements with the UK).

Which country are you currently living in?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm living in Spain at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now, regarding Alex - you mentioned that Alex doesn't live with you. Can you tell me:

1. Where does Alex currently live (which country)?
2. Who does Alex live with?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives in the UK with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex lives in the UK with their other parent, I need to understand your financial contributions.

Do you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep (such as payments for food, clothes, pocket money, or maintenance payments)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute. I help with things like clothes and other expenses for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. To determine if your contributions meet the eligibility requirements, I need to understand the amount and frequency.

Can you tell me approximately how much you contribute towards Alex's upkeep (including clothes and other expenses), and how often you make these contributions (weekly, monthly, or as a lump sum)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute weekly towards Alex's upkeep - it works out to £30 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. One more important question:

Is the other parent (who Alex lives with in the UK) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or do you know if anyone else is claiming it?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the other parent isn't claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Nobody else is getting it for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex based on what you've told me.

Based on the official UK government rules, I need to inform you of my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment of your situation, I'm afraid you would **not be eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why:

The main issue is that you don't currently live in the UK. To qualify for Child Benefit, you normally need to:
1. Live in the UK, AND
2. Be responsible for a child under 16

While you are contributing £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which demonstrates financial responsibility), and Alex does live in the UK, the fundamental requirement is that **you yourself must live in the UK** to claim Child Benefit.

There are some exceptions for people living abroad - such as Crown servants or those living in certain countries with specific agreements - but simply living in Spain as a resident doesn't qualify you for these exceptions.

**What this means:** Since Alex lives in the UK with their other parent and no one is currently claiming Child Benefit, the other parent would be eligible to claim it (assuming they meet the standard eligibility criteria). Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child, so you may want to discuss this with them.

I hope this clarifies your situation. If your circumstances change and you move back to the UK, you would be able to claim Child Benefit at that point (provided you meet the other eligibility criteria).

reasonings: To qualify for Child Benefit, you normally need to live in the UK and be responsible for a child. According to the eligibility rules, you're usually responsible for a child if either: (1) you live with them, or (2) you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. In this case, you do not live in the UK - you live in Spain - and Alex lives in the UK with their other parent, not with you. While you do contribute £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep, the main issue is that you do not live in the UK yourself. The rules state you 'normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK.' Although there are exceptions for people living abroad (such as Crown servants or those living in certain countries), simply living in Spain as a resident does not automatically qualify you for these exceptions. Since you don't meet the basic residency requirement of living in the UK, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in Spain (not in the UK) and their 6-year-old child Alex lives in the UK with the other parent. While the claimant contributes £30 weekly towards Alex's upkeep and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, the fundamental issue is that the claimant does not live in the UK. The eligibility rules require you to normally live in the UK to qualify for Child Benefit, with only specific exceptions (such as being a Crown servant or living in certain countries with agreements). The claimant does not meet the UK residency requirement and has not indicated they fall under any of the specific exceptions that would allow them to claim while living abroad.

The eligibility rules require you to normally live in the UK to qualify for Child Benefit, with only specific exceptions (such as being a Crown servant or living in certain countries with agreements). The claimant does not meet the UK residency requirement and has not indicated they fall under any of the specific exceptions that would allow them to claim while living abroad.
